narrative.weakest_link: Beat 7 is the weakest structural point: it is correctly framed as adversarial scoping, but the supporting set is small, recent, and only lightly connected to the main provenance line, so it can narrow claims but not credibly apportion causal weight among provenance, inference-time scaling, structured reasoning, and supervision choices. Beat 6 also sits adjacent to this weakness because it supports deployment feasibility rather than downstream effect.
narrative.suggested_fix: Keep the current honest scoping, but make the 6->7 handoff even more explicit in the writing outline: state that CampusGo establishes collection/provenance feasibility only, then immediately bound any later effect claim to fixed model size, fixed inference-time budget, and matched post-training procedure. In Beat 5, add a sentence that the pilot is a provenance-sensitive test under controlled conditions rather than a causal proof. If possible, add one bridge paper or explicit transition sentence connecting social-reasoning improvements from test-time methods back to why provenance claims must remain conditional.

contradictions_found: Pretraining line: the corpus does not support 'synthetic data always harms pretraining'; self-correcting/self-consuming-loop and curated synthetic-data papers show collapse is conditional and can be mitigated under controlled curation.

Pretraining line: live-web degradation is not directly demonstrated at web scale; the pipeline correctly limits this to a rising-risk claim and notes the absence of a post-2022 longitudinal contamination audit tied to downstream LM quality.

Pretraining line: filtered/documented web corpora such as RefinedWeb complicate a simple contamination-to-quality-decline story, indicating that web data quality is heterogeneous and curation can preserve performance.

Fine-tuning line: AI-feedback, self-alignment, synthetic instruction tuning, and RLAIF can match or approach RLHF on benchmarked assistant behavior, so broad claims that human-authored supervision remains universally necessary are too strong.

Fine-tuning line: benchmark gains from LIMA/Self-Instruct/Zephyr-style methods do not establish socially grounded alignment or subgroup representativeness; the pipeline explicitly notes that failure on social-reasoning tasks is not directly shown by this corpus.

Fine-tuning line: human data remains especially valuable for contested values, demographic representativeness, and opinion-sensitive tasks, with papers on opinion misalignment and human-in-the-loop RL supplying the main counterweight to synthetic-substitution claims.

Detection/provenance line: watermarking and detector papers support detectability only in restricted or cooperative settings, while paraphrase/evasion results limit any claim that current open-web pollution is broadly detectable in practice.

Cross-line tension: the thesis risks conflating pretraining contamination/collapse evidence with post-training alignment claims; the pipeline partly catches this by calling L_auth a descriptive framework rather than a validated stage-agnostic law and by noting the absence of stage-spanning validation.

Cross-line weakness: although the stage-confusion problem is acknowledged, it is not fully separated into its own contradiction program with concrete bridge tests, so the pretraining-vs-fine-tuning boundary is flagged but not exhaustively operationalized.
contradiction.verdict: Strong contradiction review overall. The pipeline surfaces major counterevidence on both independent lines, repeatedly narrows overclaims, and gives concrete handling advice instead of rhetorical certainty. It correctly attributes most tensions to the right line: collapse/web-drift/detection to pretraining, and AI-feedback/self-alignment/social-reasoning limits to fine-tuning. The main remaining weakness is cross-line handling: the danger of importing pretraining contamination evidence into fine-tuning/social-alignment conclusions is acknowledged, but not yet separated into a fully explicit contradiction track with dedicated tests and decision rules.

closest_existing_work: LIMA: Less Is More for Alignment
Whose Opinions Do Language Models Reflect?
OpenAssistant Conversations -- Democratizing Large Language Models
Position: Data Authenticity, Consent, & Provenance for AI are all broken: what will it take to fix it?
DEL-ToM: Inference-Time Scaling for Theory-of-Mind Reasoning via Dynamic Epistemic Logic
Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model
gap.gap_refinement_suggestion: The gap analysis is largely honest, but it should sharpen the paper into two separable claims: (1) CampusGo is a feasible provenance-aware collection and governance system, and (2) any downstream model claim is only a pilot-level hypothesis about data-composition effects under fixed model size and fixed inference-time budget. Strengthen Beat 5 with more explicit methodology precedent for controlled fine-tuning comparisons, ablation design, benchmark validity, and statistical evaluation; right now it is correctly labeled weak, but the methodological bridge is thinner than the rest of the writeup implies. Keep Beat 6 strictly on deployment/buildability and never as evidence of model improvement. Expand Beat 7 further with competing mechanisms from inference-time scaling, structured reasoning, verifier-guided decoding, steering, and AI-feedback/self-alignment so the final thesis is explicitly narrowed to provenance-sensitive effects after holding those factors fixed. Also quantify the collapse-risk versus mitigation literature and keep the lack of a post-2022 web-scale contamination audit front and center.

underfilled: K (5/8, highest-priority shortfall; Beat 7 is supported but thin)
补搜_queries: Category K / Beat 7: human-data indispensability for subgroup representativeness, contested values, and opinion-sensitive alignment evaluation
Category K / Beat 7: provenance-sensitive human evaluation showing AI-feedback or self-alignment does not replace community-grounded human supervision
Category K / Beat 7: demographic/subgroup audits comparing AI feedback, synthetic instruction data, and human annotator data for social alignment
Category K / Beat 7: human-authored supervision value in socially grounded reasoning beyond MT-Bench-style assistant benchmarks
Category F/G: post-training or alignment-stage evidence that data composition affects social reasoning, value pluralism, or theory-of-mind performance
Category F/G: comparisons of synthetic/AI-generated instruction data versus human-written preference or dialogue data on socially grounded tasks
Direct gap from Beats 1-2: longitudinal post-2022 web-scale contamination or authenticity audits linked to downstream LM quality
Bridge/framework gap: stage-agnostic validation of authenticity or contamination effects spanning pretraining and post-training

overselling_detected: Recursive-collapse evidence supports harm from indiscriminate recursive reuse, not from all synthetic data or from carefully curated synthetic supervision in general.
The corpus does not demonstrate broad live-web degradation; it supports measurable drift, heterogeneous quality, and rising contamination risk, while lacking a direct post-2022 web-scale audit.
L_auth is supported as a scoped descriptive framework for provenance/authenticity in fine-tuning, not as a validated general law with established predictive power.

Human-origin data is most defensibly argued to matter for socially grounded, subgroup-sensitive, and opinion-sensitive tasks; the corpus also includes credible AI-feedback and synthetic-data gains on bounded instruction-following tasks.
CampusGo is supported as feasible provenance-aware infrastructure and requirements engineering, not as validated evidence of downstream model improvement.

Inference-time scaling, structured reasoning scaffolds, supervision design, and steering remain genuine alternative explanations; the small Beat 7 literature is enough to require explicit causal restraint, not to dismiss those alternatives.
honesty.what_author_should_admit: The corpus is sufficient for a cautious, scoped paper, not a sweeping causal one. It supports a motivation story about recursive contamination risk, a narrow claim that web drift is measurable but not yet shown to be globally degrading, a descriptive L_auth framework, and a primary thesis that provenance likely matters especially for socially grounded post-training tasks under fixed model size and fixed inference-time budget. It does not justify claims that all synthetic data is harmful, that the web has already broadly degraded, that L_auth is an empirically validated law, that human data is universally indispensable, that deploying CampusGo proves model gains, or that provenance effects dominate inference-time scaling and other test-time mechanisms. The pilot should be presented as directional and hypothesis-generating, with subgroup-sensitive human evaluation and provenance-controlled ablations still needed.
